result.output:
# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Literal input characters

- When any of the follow characters added to the word, change to literal input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal Vietnamese spelling. `Z` existing in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_
- Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
- This rule is lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
Tones have a separate section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
  characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Stroke

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
  valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
  remains until the new word.
  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

- The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
  character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.

### Tones

Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
These vowel could take any tone.

#### Level (ngang)

- The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
  corresponding variants.
- If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.

#### Rising (sắc)

- Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
  -> `Ớ`.

#### Falling (huyền)

- Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
  -> `Ờ`.

#### Dipping-rising (hỏi)

- Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
  `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.

#### Rising-glottalized (ngã)

- Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
  -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.

#### Falling-glottalized (nặng)

- Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
  -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.

#### Apply rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
  position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
- If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.

#### Cancel rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
  the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Reset rules

- Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
  change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
  any tone (level), append the new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Override rules

- Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
  from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
  the word. The override tone is always successful.

#### Scan pseudo-word

- Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
  the tone in the correct position.
- The existing characters must not be empty.
- Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
- If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
  character to scan, then stop.
- If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
  backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
- After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
  `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
  `<consonant><vowels>`.

#### Tone position rules

The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:

- Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
  tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
- Exceptions:
  - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
- If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
- If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
  - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
  - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
    prioritize the first to the last in the list.
  - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
    `U` count as part of the consonant.
  - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
  - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
    consonants, put on second vowel.
  - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
- Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
- Could not put tone for other cases.

input.task: Critique a proposed test split for Vietnamese Telex tone behavior in this Zig codebase. Do not provide example code or code snippets. Focus only on test responsibilities, case selection, and risks.
input.context: The user asked for no code. I analyzed specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md and src/liblex.zig. Spec has Tones: State.add should trigger apply/cancel/reset based on S/F/R/X/J/Z; scan pseudo-word builds word/vowel bounds; tone position rules decide which vowel receives the tone. Current test `expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases` seeds State.buffer_effective, calls state.add('s'/'S'), and asserts the selected index got .rising. It contains many cases that primarily cover tone position rules: single vowels, OA/OE/OO/UY exact vs extended, GI/QU special starts, special diacritics Ơ and Ê/Â/Ô/Ă/Ư priority, suffix pseudo-word cases. There is already a separate `expect State.pseudoword will scan and provide pseudoword correctly` test covering scan boundaries. `State.apply_tone(self, word, tone)` is private but tests are in same file and can call private methods. Proposed split: create a new `State.apply_tone` unit test that constructs spans plus an explicit Pseudoword and invokes apply_tone directly to verify tone position rules independently for all non-level tones or at least a representative non-level tone, because position should be tone-agnostic. Keep `State.add apply rising tone for valid cases` as a smaller integration test proving S/s dispatch, pseudoword acquisition, no append, buffer_length unchanged, buffer_modification_index set, literal_index unchanged, mode unchanged, and a few representative positioning outcomes. Move most position cases from the integration test into the new apply_tone test; avoid duplicating cases that pseudoword test already covers purely for scan behavior. Important implementation details from src/liblex.zig: apply_tone asserts word.has_vowels(), excludes exact QU, asserts all vowels have .level, asserts tone != .level, sets target span via Span.init_diacritic_tone, and updates buffer_modification_index to the earliest modification if it was null or greater. State.add for S/s does integration checks including literal mode, empty buffer, no vowels, exact QU, no existing tone -> apply_tone, other tone -> reset+apply, same tone cancel TODO. Please identify any flaws or improvements in the proposed split and recommend a concise case-selection strategy. Again: no example code, no code blocks, no pseudocode.

run.result:
1) TL;DR

Your split is directionally right: move the tone-placement matrix into a direct `apply_tone` test, and shrink the `add('s'/'S')` test down to orchestration behavior. The main improvement I’d make is to keep a small number of end-to-end cases that still prove scanner + dispatcher composition, and to strengthen the special-vowel priority cases because your current set is too friendly.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep three clear test responsibilities.
   - `pseudoword`: only scans boundaries and vowel ranges.
   - `apply_tone`: only selects which existing vowel gets the tone and updates modification bookkeeping.
   - `add` for `S/s`: only proves dispatch/orchestration behavior.

2. Let `apply_tone` own almost all tone-position rules.
   - This is the right home for:
     - single-vowel placement
     - `GI` / non-exact `QU`
     - exact vs extended `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`
     - `Ơ` priority
     - `Ê/Â/Ô/Ă/Ư` priority
     - default “otherwise first vowel”
   - Also include `buffer_modification_index` behavior here, because it is part of the helper’s contract, not just `add`’s.

3. Keep the `add('s'/'S')` test intentionally small, but not too small.
   - Good checks to keep:
     - lowercase and uppercase trigger dispatch
     - success does not append
     - `buffer_length` unchanged
     - `buffer_modification_index` set
     - `literal_index` unchanged
     - `mode` unchanged
   - Keep a few representative successful placement outcomes, but only enough to prove the integration path still reaches the helper correctly.

4. Do not move all suffix-pseudoword cases out of integration.
   - At least one or two should stay at `add` level, specifically where the relevant pseudo-word is only the trailing suffix.
   - Otherwise you prove scanning in isolation and placement in isolation, but not that `add` wires them together correctly on real buffers.

5. Add one orchestration case that is not pure “apply”.
   - `apply_tone` cannot cover:
     - existing other tone -> reset then apply
     - same tone -> cancel, once implemented
     - no vowels -> append literal
     - exact `QU` -> append literal
   - So if you shrink `add` too much, you’ll lose coverage of the real branch logic.

3) Rationale and trade-offs

This split gives you better fault isolation:
- If a target index is wrong, `apply_tone` fails directly.
- If scan bounds are wrong, `pseudoword` fails directly.
- If trigger handling is wrong, `add` fails directly.

That is much better than a giant `add('s')` matrix that mixes scanning, rule selection, and dispatch.

The one thing not to overdo is “full isolation.” If every suffix case moves to `apply_tone`, you can accidentally mask integration bugs where `add` scans the wrong pseudo-word but `apply_tone` still works fine on a hand-constructed input.

Also, using one representative non-level tone in `apply_tone` is enough for placement logic. The position rules are tone-agnostic there. You do not need to repeat the entire matrix for all five tones.

4) Risks and guardrails

- Risk: losing end-to-end coverage.
  - Guardrail: keep 1–2 suffix cases in `add`, not just in `pseudoword`.

- Risk: hand-constructing impossible `Pseudoword` inputs.
  - Guardrail: only create shapes the scanner could realistically produce; don’t invent inconsistent bounds.

- Risk: under-testing the special-vowel priority rule.
  - This is the biggest gap in your current selection.
  - Your existing friendly case only proves one arrangement where spec priority and positional choice happen to agree.
  - Add at least one adversarial case where the higher-priority vowel appears earlier, so a “pick the rightmost special vowel” bug cannot slip through.

- Risk: forgetting helper bookkeeping.
  - Guardrail: in `apply_tone`, test `buffer_modification_index` for:
    - initially null
    - already later than the target
    - already earlier than the target

- Risk: shrinking `add` so much that override/cancel logic becomes untested.
  - Guardrail: keep one override test there once that path is stable, and a cancel test when implemented.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Move to a more systematic matrix only if:
- more tone branches (`F/R/X/J/Z`) are implemented and begin diverging,
- regressions keep appearing around priority ordering,
- or the same rule categories must be verified across many trigger branches.

Until then, a clean 3-layer split is enough. Effort: S–M.

6) Optional advanced path

If tone handling expands, the next step is a rule-category matrix:
- one shared placement matrix for `apply_tone`
- one tiny dispatch matrix per trigger in `add`
- one scan matrix for `pseudoword`

But I would not do that yet.

Concise case-selection strategy

For `pseudoword`
- Keep only structural scan cases:
  - vowels only
  - consonant + vowels
  - vowels + consonants
  - consonant + vowels + consonants
  - trailing-suffix pseudo-word
  - no-vowel suffix
- Drop cases that differ only by tone-position semantics; scanner does not care.

For `apply_tone`
- Select by rule branch, not by many word examples:
  - one single-vowel case
  - one default multi-vowel fallback case
  - one `Ơ` priority case
  - two or three special-vowel priority cases, including at least one adversarial ordering case
  - one `GI` case
  - one non-exact `QU` case
  - exact + extended for each of `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` if you want branch-level confidence
- Plus bookkeeping assertions for modification index semantics

For `add('s'/'S')`
- Keep only a small smoke set:
  - one lowercase success
  - one uppercase success
  - one trailing-suffix success
  - one no-vowel literal fallback
  - one exact-`QU` literal fallback
  - one override-existing-tone case later
  - one same-tone cancel case when implemented

Net: your split is good, but don’t make `add` so thin that it stops testing real orchestration, and do strengthen the priority-order cases because that’s where a subtle bug is most likely to survive.

129: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
130: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
131:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
132: 
133: #### Apply rules
134: 
135: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
136:   character literally.
137: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
138:   their lower cases.
139: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
140: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
141: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
142:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
143: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
144: 
145: #### Cancel rules
146: 
147: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
148:   character literally.
149: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
150:   their lower cases.
151: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
152: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
153: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
154:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
155: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
156:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
157: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
158: 
159: #### Reset rules
160: 
161: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
162:   character literally.
163: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
164: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
165: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
166:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
167: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
168:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
169: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
170:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
171: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
172: 
173: #### Override rules
174: 
175: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
176:   character literally.
177: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
178:   their lower cases.
179: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
180: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
181: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
182:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
183:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
184: 
185: #### Scan pseudo-word
186: 
187: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
188:   the tone in the correct position.
189: - The existing characters must not be empty.
190: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
191: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
192:   character to scan, then stop.
193: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
194:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
195: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
196:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
197:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
198: 
199: #### Tone position rules
200: 
201: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
202: 
203: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
204:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
205: - Exceptions:
206:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
207: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
208: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
209:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
210:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
211:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
212:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
213:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
214:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
215:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
216:     consonants, put on second vowel.
217:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
218: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
219: - Could not put tone for other cases.

progress.message:
1) TL;DR

Your split is directionally right: move the tone-placement matrix into a direct `apply_tone` test, and shrink the `add('s'/'S')` test down to orchestration behavior. The main improvement I’d make is to keep a small number of end-to-end cases that still prove scanner + dispatcher composition, and to strengthen the special-vowel priority cases because your current set is too friendly.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep three clear test responsibilities.
   - `pseudoword`: only scans boundaries and vowel ranges.
   - `apply_tone`: only selects which existing vowel gets the tone and updates modification bookkeeping.
   - `add` for `S/s`: only proves dispatch/orchestration behavior.

2. Let `apply_tone` own almost all tone-position rules.
   - This is the right home for:
     - single-vowel placement
     - `GI` / non-exact `QU`
     - exact vs extended `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`
     - `Ơ` priority
     - `Ê/Â/Ô/Ă/Ư` priority
     - default “otherwise first vowel”
   - Also include `buffer_modification_index` behavior here, because it is part of the helper’s contract, not just `add`’s.

3. Keep the `add('s'/'S')` test intentionally small, but not too small.
   - Good checks to keep:
     - lowercase and uppercase trigger dispatch
     - success does not append
     - `buffer_length` unchanged
     - `buffer_modification_index` set
     - `literal_index` unchanged
     - `mode` unchanged
   - Keep a few representative successful placement outcomes, but only enough to prove the integration path still reaches the helper correctly.

4. Do not move all suffix-pseudoword cases out of integration.
   - At least one or two should stay at `add` level, specifically where the relevant pseudo-word is only the trailing suffix.
   - Otherwise you prove scanning in isolation and placement in isolation, but not that `add` wires them together correctly on real buffers.

5. Add one orchestration case that is not pure “apply”.
   - `apply_tone` cannot cover:
     - existing other tone -> reset then apply
     - same tone -> cancel, once implemented
     - no vowels -> append literal
     - exact `QU` -> append literal
   - So if you shrink `add` too much, you’ll lose coverage of the real branch logic.

3) Rationale and trade-offs

This split gives you better fault isolation:
- If a target index is wrong, `apply_tone` fails directly.
- If scan bounds are wrong, `pseudoword` fails directly.
- If trigger handling is wrong, `add` fails directly.

That is much better than a giant `add('s')` matrix that mixes scanning, rule selection, and dispatch.

The one thing not to overdo is “full isolation.” If every suffix case moves to `apply_tone`, you can accidentally mask integration bugs where `add` scans the wrong pseudo-word but `apply_tone` still works fine on a hand-constructed input.

Also, using one representative non-level tone in `apply_tone` is enough for placement logic. The position rules are tone-agnostic there. You do not need to repeat the entire matrix for all five tones.

4) Risks and guardrails

- Risk: losing end-to-end coverage.
  - Guardrail: keep 1–2 suffix cases in `add`, not just in `pseudoword`.

- Risk: hand-constructing impossible `Pseudoword` inputs.
  - Guardrail: only create shapes the scanner could realistically produce; don’t invent inconsistent bounds.

- Risk: under-testing the special-vowel priority rule.
  - This is the biggest gap in your current selection.
  - Your existing friendly case only proves one arrangement where spec priority and positional choice happen to agree.
  - Add at least one adversarial case where the higher-priority vowel appears earlier, so a “pick the rightmost special vowel” bug cannot slip through.

- Risk: forgetting helper bookkeeping.
  - Guardrail: in `apply_tone`, test `buffer_modification_index` for:
    - initially null
    - already later than the target
    - already earlier than the target

- Risk: shrinking `add` so much that override/cancel logic becomes untested.
  - Guardrail: keep one override test there once that path is stable, and a cancel test when implemented.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Move to a more systematic matrix only if:
- more tone branches (`F/R/X/J/Z`) are implemented and begin diverging,
- regressions keep appearing around priority ordering,
- or the same rule categories must be verified across many trigger branches.

Until then, a clean 3-layer split is enough. Effort: S–M.

6) Optional advanced path

If tone handling expands, the next step is a rule-category matrix:
- one shared placement matrix for `apply_tone`
- one tiny dispatch matrix per trigger in `add`
- one scan matrix for `pseudoword`

But I would not do that yet.

Concise case-selection strategy

For `pseudoword`
- Keep only structural scan cases:
  - vowels only
  - consonant + vowels
  - vowels + consonants
  - consonant + vowels + consonants
  - trailing-suffix pseudo-word
  - no-vowel suffix
- Drop cases that differ only by tone-position semantics; scanner does not care.

For `apply_tone`
- Select by rule branch, not by many word examples:
  - one single-vowel case
  - one default multi-vowel fallback case
  - one `Ơ` priority case
  - two or three special-vowel priority cases, including at least one adversarial ordering case
  - one `GI` case
  - one non-exact `QU` case
  - exact + extended for each of `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` if you want branch-level confidence
- Plus bookkeeping assertions for modification index semantics

For `add('s'/'S')`
- Keep only a small smoke set:
  - one lowercase success
  - one uppercase success
  - one trailing-suffix success
  - one no-vowel literal fallback
  - one exact-`QU` literal fallback
  - one override-existing-tone case later
  - one same-tone cancel case when implemented

Net: your split is good, but don’t make `add` so thin that it stops testing real orchestration, and do strengthen the priority-order cases because that’s where a subtle bug is most likely to survive.

No code was written.

## Proposed approach

Split the current tone coverage into three layers with distinct responsibilities:

1. **`State.pseudoword` test**
   - Owns pseudo-word scanning only.
   - It should verify `word.start`, `word.end`, `vowels_start`, `vowels_end`, and `length`.
   - It should not try to prove tone placement rules.

2. **New `State.apply_tone` test**
   - Owns tone position rules.
   - It should seed `buffer_effective`, construct a valid `Pseudoword`, call `apply_tone` directly, and verify:
     - which vowel receives the tone,
     - all other spans remain unchanged,
     - case/diacritic are preserved,
     - `buffer_modification_index` is updated correctly.
   - This avoids routing every placement rule through `State.add('s')`.

3. **Reduced `State.add apply rising tone` test**
   - Owns integration only:
     - `S` / `s` dispatch reaches tone application,
     - no new character is appended on successful apply,
     - `buffer_length` stays unchanged,
     - `buffer_modification_index` is set,
     - `literal_index` and `mode` stay unchanged,
     - pseudo-word scan and `apply_tone` compose correctly for representative cases.

## Cases to move from “apply rising tone” into new `apply_tone` test

Move most of the placement matrix from [`expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L2158-L2292), especially cases that exist only to prove tone position rules:

- single vowel placement,
- multiple vowel default placement,
- exact `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`,
- extended `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`,
- `GI` special start,
- `QU` special start when followed by more vowels,
- `Ơ` priority,
- `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` priority,
- suffix pseudo-word placement cases, except for one or two representative integration cases.

The new test should be organized by spec rule branch, not by many word examples.

## Cases to keep in “apply rising tone”

Keep a small smoke/integration set. Suggested minimum:

- one lowercase trigger success, e.g. `s`,
- one uppercase trigger success, e.g. `S`,
- one simple single-vowel or consonant+vowel case,
- one multi-vowel case proving `State.add` reaches placement logic,
- one trailing-suffix pseudo-word case proving scanner + add + apply compose correctly.

Do not keep every `OA` / `UY` / diacritic priority case there; that duplicates `apply_tone`.

Also keep or add separate `State.add` tests for branches that `apply_tone` cannot cover:

- no vowels → append literal,
- exact `QU` → append literal,
- override existing different tone → reset then apply,
- same-tone cancel once implemented.

## Important Oracle feedback

Oracle agreed with the split, with these refinements:

- Do not make `State.add` too thin. Keep at least one or two suffix pseudo-word integration cases, because otherwise scanning and placement are tested independently but not together.
- `apply_tone` should also test `buffer_modification_index`, because that is part of the helper’s contract.
- Use one non-level tone for the placement matrix; the placement rules are tone-agnostic. Repeating the full matrix for all tones would be unnecessary.
- Add adversarial special-vowel priority cases. The current friendly examples can miss bugs where priority order and scan order accidentally align.

## Notable risk found during analysis

The spec says the special-vowel priority order is:

`Ê` before `Â` before `Ô` before `Ă` before `Ư`.

The current implementation appears to scan the vowel run from right to left and records the first matching special vowel. That means a case like “higher-priority special vowel earlier, lower-priority special vowel later” may expose a bug. The current test has `ăê`, which is friendly because both right-to-left scan order and spec priority choose `ê`.

The new `apply_tone` test should include at least one adversarial priority case where the expected vowel follows the spec priority rather than scan order. This is exactly the kind of rule-level issue that belongs in `apply_tone`, not in the integration test.

My comments:

- Accept the scan direction in the production code. I will change the spec, because these characters don't compose together in formal Vitenamese.
- Add a case in "apply rising tone" where the different tone exists in first vowel, this can happen when user type half of the word and put tone, then continue with more vowels, then override tone.
- For apply_tone we should iterate the cases with all tones (exclude level).
- I agreed with others.

Let me preview you plan.
